workoutSplits.name: Full Body (1 Day/Week)
workoutSplits.description: One comprehensive full-body workout per week hitting all major muscle groups. Only recommended for absolute beginners or those with severe time constraints. Frequency is very low, limiting muscle growth.

weeklySchedule.description: One day per week (any day). Six rest days for recovery.

pros: Minimum time commitment (one session/week)
Full week recovery between sessions
Works for complete beginners
Introductory to strength training
cons: Very low muscle-building frequency (1x per week)
Very long session (90+ minutes)
Insufficient volume for adaptation
Not suitable for progression
Risk of detraining between sessions
workoutSplits.notes: Only suitable for absolute beginners or those with extreme time constraints. Frequency is too low for meaningful progress. Consider 2+ days/week as soon as possible.

workoutSplits.name: Full Body (2 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Two full-body workouts per week with alternating focus. Best for beginners building a routine or intermediate lifters with limited time. Two days per week is minimum viable frequency for muscle building.

weeklySchedule.description: Alternate between dayA and dayB with at least one rest day between. Example: Mon (dayA), rest, Wed (dayB), rest, Fri optional second dayA

pros: Minimum viable frequency (2x per week per muscle)
Sessions are shorter (45–60 minutes)
Works well with busy schedules
Adequate recovery between sessions
Good for beginners learning consistency
cons: Only 1x per week frequency for each muscle (some science suggests 2x is optimal)
Limited volume compared to higher frequency
Still relatively low frequency for advanced lifters
Plateau risk after initial adaptation
workoutSplits.notes: Minimum recommended frequency for meaningful muscle growth. Works best paired with a goal like muscle_gain or general_fitness.

workoutSplits.name: Full Body (3 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Hit all muscle groups in each session. Best for beginners learning movement patterns and intermediate lifters with limited time. Three sessions per week with at least one rest day between workouts.

weeklySchedule.description: Monday, Wednesday, Friday (or any Mon/Wed/Fri schedule). At least one rest day between workouts. Three rest days per week for recovery.

pros: Great for beginners learning movement patterns
Only 3 days per week is sustainable long-term
Full recovery between sessions
Builds balanced strength across all muscles
Works well with busy schedules
Lower risk of overuse injuries
Sessions are manageable (45–60 min)
cons: Only 2x per week frequency per muscle group
Limited total volume for advanced lifters
Can be fatiguing doing full body each session
Plateau after initial adaptation
workoutSplits.notes: Sweet spot for most people. Balances frequency, volume, and sustainability. Perfect for Mon/Wed/Fri schedule.

workoutSplits.name: Upper/Lower (4 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Separate upper body and lower body days. Train each muscle group twice per week. The classic 4-day split: Upper, Lower, Rest, Upper, Lower, Rest, Rest. Excellent for intermediate lifters balancing frequency and recovery.

weeklySchedule.description: Upper, Lower, Rest, Upper, Lower, Rest, Rest. Example: Monday (Upper A), Tuesday (Lower A), Wednesday (Rest), Thursday (Upper B), Friday (Lower B), Saturday (Rest), Sunday (Rest)

pros: Excellent muscle-building frequency (2x per week per muscle)
High volume with adequate recovery
Perfect for intermediate lifters
Sessions are focused (upper vs lower)
Session length is manageable (55–70 min)
Great for both strength and hypertrophy
Very effective for fat loss with muscle preservation
cons: Requires 4 days per week commitment
Still only 4 days (some advanced lifters prefer 5+)
Less flexibility than 3-day splits for scheduling
workoutSplits.notes: The classic intermediate split. Balances frequency, volume, intensity, and recovery. Works exceptionally well for lifters with 1–3 years of training experience.

workoutSplits.name: Upper/Lower (5 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Upper/Lower split with an additional full-body or conditioning day. Train upper and lower twice per week with extra volume or conditioning work. Higher volume and frequency for advanced lifters.

weeklySchedule.description: Upper, Lower, Upper, Lower, Conditioning. Example: Monday (Upper A), Tuesday (Lower A), Wednesday (Upper B), Thursday (Lower B), Friday (Conditioning), Saturday (Rest), Sunday (Rest)

pros: Excellent muscle-building frequency (2x per week per muscle)
High total volume with extra conditioning
Extra recovery day (dayE is optional/conditioning)
Great for fat loss and muscle gain simultaneously
Additional conditioning improves work capacity
Flexible dayE (can be conditioning, mobility, or accessories)
Perfect for athletic performance goals
cons: Requires 5 days per week (demanding schedule)
Higher total volume may require more nutrition
Requires strong recovery (sleep, nutrition)
Risk of overtraining if not managed well
workoutSplits.notes: Ideal for intermediate lifters wanting extra volume or conditioning. Day E is flexible: can be light conditioning, mobility, or extra accessory work based on weaknesses.

workoutSplits.name: Push/Pull/Legs (5 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Rotate through Push, Pull, and Legs with Push and Pull repeated. Each muscle group trained twice per week with very high volume. Advanced split for experienced lifters wanting maximum hypertrophy.

weeklySchedule.description: Push, Pull, Legs, Push, Pull. Rest 2 days. Example: Monday (Push A), Tuesday (Pull A), Wednesday (Legs A), Thursday (Push B), Friday (Pull B), Saturday (Rest), Sunday (Rest)

pros: Excellent upper body frequency (2x per week)
Very high upper body volume for hypertrophy
Clear workout focus (push/pull/legs)
Works exceptionally well for muscle gain
Experienced lifters love this split
Only 5 days per week (2 rest days)
Sessions are manageable at 55–75 min
cons: Legs only 1x per week (lower frequency)
Very high volume requires excellent recovery
Demanding for natural lifters
Limited conditioning work
Not ideal for fat loss (high calorie demand)
workoutSplits.notes: The classic 5-day PPL split. Excellent for hypertrophy-focused advanced lifters. Upper body gets 2x/week frequency while legs get 1x/week, which is a tradeoff for higher upper body volume.

workoutSplits.name: Push/Pull/Legs (6 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Full Push/Pull/Legs split hitting each muscle group twice per week. The most complete PPL iteration with balanced frequency and volume across all body parts. For serious advanced lifters with strong recovery habits.

weeklySchedule.description: Push, Pull, Legs, Push, Pull, Legs. Rest 1 day. Example: Monday (Push A), Tuesday (Pull A), Wednesday (Legs A), Thursday (Push B), Friday (Pull B), Saturday (Legs B), Sunday (Rest)

pros: Perfect frequency for all muscles (2x per week)
Excellent balance of push, pull, and legs volume
Highest total training volume in any split
Best for pure hypertrophy gains
All body parts get equal focus
Legs get same frequency as upper body
The most complete PPL iteration
cons: Requires 6 days per week commitment (only 1 rest day)
Extremely high volume (98 sets/week)
Demands excellent nutrition and recovery
Minimal conditioning work
Not ideal for fat loss
May lead to overtraining if recovery is poor
Requires experienced lifters with strong discipline
workoutSplits.notes: The ultimate PPL split for dedicated advanced lifters. Requires top-tier nutrition, sleep, and recovery. Best reserved for users training 2+ years. One full rest day per week is critical for adaptation.

workoutSplits.name: Body Part (4 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Each day focuses on one or two muscle groups with high volume. Chest/Back, Legs, Shoulders, Arms. Great for advanced lifters wanting to specialize in specific areas.

weeklySchedule.description: Chest/Back, Legs, Shoulders, Arms. Example: Monday (Chest/Back), Tuesday (Legs), Wednesday (Shoulders), Thursday (Arms), Friday (Rest), Saturday (Rest), Sunday (Rest)

pros: High volume per muscle group (full day dedicated)
Good for specialization (can focus on lagging parts)
Only 4 days per week (3 rest days)
Sessions are focused on one area
Works well for muscle imbalances
cons: Very low frequency per muscle (1x per week only)
Requires a week to hit all body parts
Suboptimal for hypertrophy (frequency is low)
Recovery between muscle groups is long
Not ideal for beginners (needs experience)
workoutSplits.notes: Body part split with low frequency. Best for advanced lifters who can handle high volume per session and have strong recovery. Lower frequency limits hypertrophy potential compared to UL or PPL.

workoutSplits.name: Body Part (5 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Each muscle group gets its own dedicated day. Chest, Back, Legs, Shoulders, Arms. Maximum volume for each body part with focused training.

weeklySchedule.description: Chest, Back, Legs, Shoulders, Arms. Example: Monday (Chest), Tuesday (Back), Wednesday (Legs), Thursday (Shoulders), Friday (Arms), Saturday (Rest), Sunday (Rest)

pros: Highest volume for each muscle group
Perfect focus — each day one body part
Great for specialization and weak points
Sessions are very focused
Works well for experienced lifters
cons: Very low frequency per muscle (1x per week only)
Suboptimal for hypertrophy (science suggests 2x/week is better)
Recovery between muscle group training is long
Plateau risk after initial adaptation
Not ideal for simultaneous strength and size goals
workoutSplits.notes: Pure body part split with maximum volume per muscle. Only for very experienced lifters with strong recovery. Low frequency (1x/week) limits optimal hypertrophy despite high volume.

workoutSplits.name: Body Part (6 Days/Week)
workoutSplits.description: Each muscle group gets dedicated days with some getting double days. Chest, Back, Legs x2, Shoulders, Arms. Combines high volume with improved frequency for key areas.

weeklySchedule.description: Chest, Back, Legs (Quads), Shoulders, Legs (Hinge), Arms. Rest 1 day. Example: Monday (Chest), Tuesday (Back), Wednesday (Legs-Quads), Thursday (Shoulders), Friday (Legs-Hinge), Saturday (Arms), Sunday (Rest)

pros: High volume for all muscle groups
Legs get 2x per week frequency (better than 1x)
Glutes get 2x per week for better hypertrophy
Maintains full body specialization focus
Only 1 rest day (high frequency training)
cons: Most muscle groups still 1x per week (suboptimal for hypertrophy)
Very demanding (6 days/week with only 1 rest day)
Extremely high volume (101 sets/week)
Requires elite recovery (sleep, nutrition, supplements)
Risk of overtraining if recovery is poor
Not suitable for natural lifters with normal recovery
workoutSplits.notes: High-frequency body part split with legs split across two sessions. Best for advanced lifters with excellent recovery and lifestyle. High volume and frequency demand top-tier nutrition and sleep. Only 1 rest day requires strong discipline.
